mnemonics.title: Wetland Types Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: M-S-B-F-M: Marsh (grasses), Swamp (trees), Bog (Sphagnum moss), Fen (grasses/sedges), Mangrove (coastal trees)

mnemonics.title: Wetland Functions
mnemonics.content: W.E.T. - Water purification, Erosion control, Tidal buffering (flood control)

acronyms.expansion: Regional Approach to Managing Sustainable Aquatic Resources
acronyms.usage: Remember this relates to wetland conservation convention - actually stands for the city in Iran where treaty was signed

acronyms.expansion: Water-Logged Ecosystems Temporarily Existing Between Land and water Domains

acronyms.usage: Bogs have acidic, nutrient-poor conditions supporting mainly moss growth

visualTips.visualization: Picture a sponge in a bucket - wetlands hold water like a sponge, releasing it slowly. The water level rises and falls (hydroperiod) determining what can grow there.
Think of bogs as 'rain-fed' (like a rooftop collecting rain) and fens as 'groundwater-fed' (like a spring). Rainwater is pure and acidic; groundwater has minerals and is less acidic.
Marsh = 'M' for Many herbaceous plants (soft stems). Swamp = 'S' for Shrubs/trees (woody stems). Both need water, different vegetation.

analogies.analogy: Just as kidneys filter waste from blood and produce urine, wetlands filter pollutants from water and release cleaner water downstream.
Wetlands absorb excess water during floods and release it slowly during dry periods, like a sponge soaking up and releasing water.

analogies.analanalogy: Wetlands provide 'rooms' (different habitats) for countless species - birds, fish, amphibians, insects - like a hotel housing many guests.

analogies.analogy: Wetlands store carbon in their soils like money in a bank - taking carbon from the atmosphere and keeping it locked away for long periods.
